title: From Grief to Grit: How This Widow Turned Loss Into Leadership Success
guestBio: Leslie Christen is a powerhouse business owner, mother of two boys, and widow who has transformed personal loss into entrepreneurial strength. As one of the original founding members of the Office Hours Girls Club, she exemplifies how women can rise above grief and doubt to lead with both soul and strength.
excerpt: When life hits you with unimaginable loss, how do you keep moving forward? Leslie Christen shares her raw journey from grief to grit, revealing how she rebuilt her life as a widow, mother, and business owner while navigating the invisible labor that holds it all together.
metaDescription: Discover how Leslie Christen turned grief into grit, building a successful business as a widow and mom while leading with heart and soul.
keyTakeaways: The invisible labor of motherhood becomes even more complex when you're carrying it all alone after loss.
Being underestimated in business can actually become your secret weapon for success.
Technology and AI tools can be life-saving resources for overwhelmed mothers and entrepreneurs.
Sisterhood and community support are essential for women rebuilding their lives after tragedy.
Leading with soul and authenticity creates deeper connections than traditional business approaches.
Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward.
The patriarchal business world often undervalues women's emotional intelligence and heart-centered leadership.

pullQuotes.text: Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward.

timestamps.label: Life-Saving Tech for Moms & Business Owners
What's Next for Leslie + Favorite Comforts

content:
When Aimee Rickabus sits down with her longtime friend Leslie Christen, you immediately feel the warmth of two women who've weathered life's storms together. But this isn't just a conversation between old friends from Laguna Beach High School—it's a masterclass in how to transform your deepest pain into your greatest strength.

Leslie's story is one that will stop you in your tracks. As a widow, mother of two boys, successful business owner, and founding member of the Office Hours Girls Club, she embodies what it means to rise above circumstances that could have easily broken her spirit. Instead, she's chosen to lead with both grit and heart, showing us all what's possible when you refuse to let life's hardest moments define your limits.

The Weight of Invisible Labor After Loss

The conversation takes a profound turn when Leslie opens up about the invisible labor of motherhood—that endless mental and emotional work that keeps families functioning—and how it becomes exponentially more complex when you're carrying it all alone. You know this feeling, don't you? That constant mental checklist, the emotional regulation, the countless decisions that no one else even notices you're making.

For Leslie, this invisible labor took on new dimensions after becoming a widow. Suddenly, she wasn't just managing the typical load—she was holding space for her children's grief while processing her own, making business decisions while navigating single parenthood, and rebuilding an entire life structure from the ground up. It's the kind of strength that doesn't make headlines but deserves every bit of recognition we can give it.

What strikes you most about Leslie's approach is her honesty about the struggle. She doesn't pretend it's easy or that she has it all figured out. Instead, she gives you permission to acknowledge that some seasons of life require you to dig deeper than you ever thought possible, and that's not just okay—it's human.

Turning Underestimation Into Your Secret Weapon

One of the most powerful moments in their conversation comes when Leslie discusses how being underestimated in the business world has actually become her secret weapon. As women, we've all felt that sting of not being taken seriously, of having our ideas dismissed or our capabilities questioned. But Leslie has found a way to flip that script entirely.

When people underestimate you, she explains, it gives you the freedom to exceed their expectations in ways that create genuine surprise and lasting impact. While others are focused on proving themselves through traditional markers of success, you can be building something deeper, more sustainable, and more aligned with your values.

This perspective shift is everything. Instead of exhausting yourself trying to prove your worth to people who may never see it, you can channel that energy into creating results that speak for themselves. It's not about being defensive or trying to change minds—it's about being so focused on your mission that external opinions become irrelevant background noise.

The Life-Saving Power of Smart Systems

The conversation takes a practical turn when Leslie shares the technology and systems that have become non-negotiable in managing her multifaceted life. From AI applications that help streamline business processes to organizational tools that keep family life running smoothly, she's discovered that the right tech stack isn't just helpful—it's life-saving for overwhelmed mothers and entrepreneurs.

What's refreshing about Leslie's approach is how she views technology as a tool for creating more space for what matters most, rather than just another thing to manage. She's intentional about choosing systems that actually reduce her mental load rather than adding to it, and she's not afraid to invest in solutions that give her back precious time and energy.

This isn't about having the latest gadgets or following every productivity trend. It's about being strategic and honest about where you need support, then finding the tools that actually deliver on their promises. Leslie's willingness to experiment and adapt her systems shows that managing it all doesn't mean doing it all manually.

Leading with Soul in a Heart-Starved World

Perhaps the most compelling aspect of Leslie's journey is how she's chosen to lead with soul and authenticity in a business world that often rewards the opposite. Rather than hardening herself or adopting traditionally masculine approaches to success, she's doubled down on the very qualities that make her unique—her empathy, her emotional intelligence, and her ability to create genuine connections.

This kind of leadership isn't always easy or immediately rewarded, but it creates something that traditional business approaches often miss: lasting relationships built on trust and mutual respect. Leslie's clients don't just hire her for her expertise—they hire her because they know she genuinely cares about their success and will bring her whole self to the partnership.

The ripple effects of this approach extend far beyond business. When you lead with soul, you give others permission to do the same. You create spaces where authenticity is valued over perfection, where vulnerability is seen as strength, and where success is measured not just in dollars but in impact and fulfillment.

The Power of Sisterhood and Community

Throughout their conversation, the importance of sisterhood and community support weaves through every topic. Leslie's involvement with the Office Hours Girls Club isn't just a networking opportunity—it's a lifeline that connects her with other women who understand the unique challenges of building something meaningful while managing all of life's demands.

This kind of community becomes even more crucial when you're rebuilding after loss. Having women in your corner who can celebrate your wins, hold space for your struggles, and remind you of your strength when you forget—that's not a luxury, it's a necessity. Leslie's story is a testament to what becomes possible when women support each other without reservation or competition.

The message is clear: you don't have to carry it all alone, and you shouldn't try to. Building a network of women who truly understand your journey isn't just about getting support—it's about creating the kind of community that lifts everyone higher and makes success feel more sustainable and meaningful.

Leslie Christen's story reminds us that our most difficult chapters don't have to be our final ones. With the right mindset, systems, and support, we can transform our deepest challenges into our greatest sources of strength and use that strength to lead in ways that honor both our struggles and our dreams.

quiz.title: How Do You Rise From Life's Hardest Moments?
quiz.description: Discover your unique approach to turning challenges into strength, inspired by Leslie Christen's journey from grief to powerful leadership.

questions.question: When you're facing an overwhelming challenge, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Take a deep breath and tackle it one small step at a time
Call someone you trust to talk through it
Research solutions and make a strategic plan
Trust your gut and dive in, knowing you'll figure it out

questions.question: How do you handle it when people underestimate you?

options.text: Let your results speak louder than their assumptions
Find allies who see your true value and capabilities
Use their low expectations as an advantage to surprise them
Stay true to yourself regardless of their opinions

questions.question: What does 'invisible labor' mean to you in your daily life?

options.text: The emotional work of keeping everything running smoothly
Being the person others turn to for support and guidance
Managing all the behind-the-scenes logistics nobody sees
Carrying the mental load while still showing up authentically

questions.question: When building something new in your life, you tend to focus on:

options.text: Creating something meaningful that aligns with your values
Building connections and relationships that will last
Finding efficient systems and tools to make it sustainable
Staying true to your vision even if it's unconventional

questions.question: Your approach to showing vulnerability in leadership is:

options.text: Share your struggles in ways that help others feel less alone
Create safe spaces where everyone can be honest about their challenges
Be strategic about when and how you show your human side
Lead with your whole self, including the messy parts

questions.question: What gives you the most energy when rebuilding after a setback?

options.text: Knowing that your perseverance inspires others quietly
Having a strong support network cheering you on
Seeing your strategic moves start to pay off
Staying aligned with who you really are through the process

results.title: The Quiet Revolutionary
results.description: You transform pain into purpose through steady, behind-the-scenes action. While others might not always see your struggles, your authentic approach to rebuilding creates ripple effects that change everything around you. You lead with your heart while maintaining incredible inner strength. Leslie's story of turning invisible labor into visible success will show you how your quiet power is actually your greatest leadership asset.

results.title: The Community Catalyst
results.description: You instinctively know that healing happens in connection with others. When life gets hard, you both seek and create the sisterhood that sustains you through anything. Your vulnerability becomes a bridge that helps others feel less alone. Hearing how Leslie built her support network and founded meaningful communities will inspire you to see how your natural gift for connection is a superpower in both business and life.

results.title: The Strategic Phoenix
results.description: You rise from challenges by getting resourceful and finding innovative solutions. Whether it's leveraging technology, thinking outside the box, or turning limitations into advantages, you refuse to be defined by circumstances. Your resilience shows up as creative problem-solving. Leslie's approach to using every tool available and turning being underestimated into a secret weapon will resonate deeply with your strategic mindset.

results.title: The Authentic Trailblazer
results.description: You lead by example, showing others what's possible when you refuse to dim your light despite life's hardships. Your courage to be vulnerable while staying strong creates space for others to do the same. You're not afraid to challenge systems that don't serve you. Leslie's journey of leading with soul in a patriarchal business world will validate your instinct that authentic leadership is the most powerful kind.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in 8 figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm so excited to have Leslie Christen here, my Laguna Beach High School sister, a powerhouse business owner and a mom to two incredible boys. Leslie's the heart behind Laguna Embroidery, crafting custom apparel with soul and an original founder of our office hours girls club. Snacks, chats, and all the vibes every other Friday. As a widow, she's turned grief into grit, building a legacy from her garage into a thriving shop in Laguna Beach. Hey Leslie.

**Leslie:** Hello.

**Aimee:** So glad to have you on today. Thanks for coming in for me. I really appreciate it, sister. So I don't know if you got a chance to look at chapter one, but one of my main things when I was writing the book was digging into the idea of the invisible labor, the invisible work that women carry for society as a whole. How do you feel like you're impacted by the invisible work in your household, in your business?

**Leslie:** Well incredibly. I mean I am a single mom. I'm a widow. I've had to really step into positions that people shouldn't have to step into, right? I've lost my partner. I've lost a lot of things. So essentially, since we're talking business, I mean I started my business and then a week later, my husband died of suicide.

**Aimee:** Oh whoa.

**Leslie:** So I mean, I stepped into invisible labor almost instantly. I got to pick up everything.

**Aimee:** That's amazing that you had the resilience to power through and continue to adjust and conquer with your business, you know.

**Leslie:** Well it was May 2020. So we were coming out of COVID at that point. Still lingering from the whole shutdown and everything. And building a business during that time and reshaping, things getting back to normal at that point.

**Aimee:** And your husband was an amazing guy who went to high school with us too. Amazing human being who just suffered from mental health and depression and it's so quiet labor in his own mind. It's so sad that we're still losing people to things like that, you know, but you're able to take your grief when you had your kids to take care of. I mean, when we're moms, it's like we deal with hard stuff, but at the same time, we're committed to these little people and we're so worried about them.

**Leslie:** Well yeah. I mean, I think one of the biggest issues that I have is that I wasn't allowed to grieve because you're helping everyone else grieve. So I mean, that has been a big point in my life in the last three years. We have to put our feelings on hold a lot of the time in order to do the invisible labor of carrying the emotional burden for our children.

**Aimee:** Whether you're going through a divorce, which I've done, or whether you're going through some other really big life stuff, we pick it up. We pick it up and then it's almost like I feel like I just have to put it in a box and be like that later. There's no other choice. You have to keep going, you know. So you basically turned your grief into grit and started with the embroidery.

**Leslie:** Yeah. It was an interesting dynamic. I mean, essentially, here I am launching a company, he passes. What do I do at that point? Of course I wanted to curl up in a ball and do nothing, but I had no choice. I was raising my two sons. I wanted to see this company through. And so I just powered on. There was no other choice.

But the people that I surrounded myself with and the people that came through for me essentially helped me survive. They saved me. My little embroidery shop is in a store in Laguna Beach. It's called Good Together House. So I have a little section in the back. It's like a little atelier. And the woman who owns it I've been friends with for years. But I essentially went into her store and there's all these women that work there. So I mean they got to meet me at a time that was incredibly difficult.

**Aimee:** You were very vulnerable.

**Leslie:** Exactly. And they allowed me without even really knowing me. They allowed me to cry or grieve or be there for me, build up my company, took orders for me, really filled in the void when I needed it. And my business is only great today because of these women stepping up for me.

**Aimee:** That's what The Manager community is all about is how can we step up for each other as women, building community. Women are stronger together. Through the millennia it's always been women have always been in the circles together. Women work better when we're in cooperation with each other.

Competition and us being the first version of feminism, I feel like we were trying to imitate men and being competitive with each other and judging each other and that bitchiness, that ugly idea that we have about women. I feel like that's such an old version of that's where we were, but that's not really what we've ever been through the millennia. And through the millennia, we've been such the sisterhood in the red tent, nursing each other's babies and helping each other give birth and just in the grit, up in it with each other. And I think that we as a whole, as a society, need to get back to that. And it seems like we are moving in that direction.

**Leslie:** I mean, you have to choose who you surround yourself with. It's almost like a toxic relationship. Either you can stay in a group of women that aren't there for you or you can choose better, and we all have the ability to choose that and leave things and start fresh.

**Aimee:** Finding your having the strength to do that too. And it's not always easy, you know. As you grow in your adult life, as you become an adult and you start to value yourself and know yourself better, then you choose better people for yourself.

**Leslie:** I mean, I was flung into that. I met 13 women that I never thought that I would know before. And it was almost like meant to be at that time.

**Aimee:** It very much was. It was like you had your angels on your shoulders and they just were guiding you through this thing. That's amazing. And then so you're doing it all by yourself and it seems like you're pretty much in a woman's world here, but have you ever come up against the patriarchal wound? Being underestimated or being slighted for being female? Having people not think that you're capable of running a business. Have you run into that at all?

**Leslie:** I chose very women-centered careers. So before my embroidery company, I was a stylist for 20 years. So I worked with lots of women. My clients were women. The PR people I worked with were women. The magazine editors were women. I had surrounded myself with a lot of women. So I can't really attest to anyone saying you can't do it. And I'm also someone who if someone says I can't do it, then I'm most definitely going to do it. I don't think that that has ever truly affected me. And then now I'm in a shop with a whole bunch of women. Most of my clients are women.

I think most of the people that I've been let down with for marriage or family, men in that, and then in your travels. You guys traveled extensively. About five years we traveled. I've lived in Croatia, Costa Rica, Egypt, Greece. My sons have been to 16 different countries. So we've traveled extensively and it was an interesting dynamic to be 38 years old and coming from a very sheltered community coming from Laguna Beach and not really knowing, thinking I knew a lot about things but then seeing the dynamics around the world.

Being in Egypt in 115 degree weather and women were wearing black burkas and the men were wearing white, just the color change of the weather, a flowy kind of breathable outfit and the women are covered head to toe in hot black. And then in Uganda just seeing women walk miles and miles for water and how to support their family and the men essentially were not allowed, they didn't do that much. Maybe they worked maybe they didn't, but women really took over that community, those communities and did a lot more of the heavy lifting.

**Aimee:** Most certainly. Of the labor.

**Leslie:** I mean, you see these girls walking with jugs of water on their head. And then my vision of the men in the community were very demanding, but yet they didn't do much, right? So they're just sitting around shooting with their buddies drinking some sort of sewing circle. It was men sitting around drinking this kind of porridge alcohol type thing. Or the manager, the owner of a hotel, directing all these women to do stuff, being very dictatorish. So it was interesting at that point in my life because things were shifting in my life and it was just interesting to see and kind of be slapped in the face with reality on that too.

**Aimee:** The gender reality, the international gender reality because I would say that I probably wasn't in a, I didn't have that around me. Laguna was a bubble. Absolutely. It's a very privileged and beautiful place to grow up. We had amazing childhoods and we had a lot of equality with the boys that we grew up with. We really did.

I was talking with a friend yesterday and she was talking about her childhood growing up in India and how she wasn't allowed to go to the movies or go out with her friends. She had to lie to her mother about playing table tennis. She'd go to school early and play table tennis and she was one of the best players and they wanted her to compete for the school. So the coach called her mom and said, "Hey, she's amazing. Can she play for the team and compete for the school next Sunday?" And her mom just said, "Oh, she's playing table tennis." No, absolutely not.

Actually, at that time, it wasn't even becoming a doctor. They basically just wanted her to be a wife. So her mom had her take embroidery lessons and how to do nice little quiet things and sew clothes and how to cook and all of the appropriate things that women did 100 years ago in this country.

**Leslie:** And how old is she?

**Aimee:** She's like 50. I'm 47. She's our age. And that was her childhood growing up. It's kind of striking when you compare it. Today is International Women's Day. And it's like when you look at the global experience, the international experience of being a woman, we're so lucky to be here.

**Leslie:** But we're not smart enough. A lot of my girlfriends and friends do a lot of activism and they do a lot of stuff, but they have no idea the things that are out in the world. The real world. The third world. Where people are dying, where people are facing adversity. There's a lot of performative type things, and that's all right. Please get involved, do stuff. We've had a charity that I felt, we've done all those type of things, but you have to know the scope of everything that is happening around the world. And if you did, then you'd probably be paralyzed with fear and sadness.

**Aimee:** Where do you start? It's such a big problem. I think that it's more like eating a whale and it's one bite at a time kind of a thing. It's one woman at a time. It's how can we help one and how can she help the next one? And it's just a chain reaction of as the freed get freed can they free one more? And that's sort of my beautiful hopeful mission on this journey is how can we help each other, how can we just raise awareness about it and get involved and just talk about the issues, have these conversations that nice girls aren't supposed to have, take up space.

**Leslie:** Absolutely. It's still a global issue. We'll figure it out. The more collective minds we have on this problem, one of us is going to have a great idea. And that's all it takes.

**Aimee:** We all have great ideas. We all do. And we're such problem solvers. So it's like, okay, let's first you have to admit you have a problem if you want to solve it, right? So let's admit we have a problem and then let's figure it out. Come on girls, we got this. I know we do.

So in your business, chapter one's all about technology and how this revolution in technology revolution is going to be amazing. But for you personally in the last maybe 5 years since digital transformation hit, what's been your biggest go-to as far as apps that you're using in your business or helping you to manage your kids, your household, ordering stuff for the house. What are you doing?

**Leslie:** I think I'm a little late to the game, but I have just been using ChatGPT probably the last year and it's significantly changed my business and my life for the better. Whether it's I was like I want to go to Mexico City for seven days, could you plan an itinerary for me and the airfare and the hotels and all that kind of stuff? There's one thing that I hate and that's planning trips. That's such a mind load. But it's just the mental load of doing everything.

And I'm such a nerd. I used to read every travel book on the country before I went to make sure I didn't miss anything, and now you can go on your ChatGPT and just be like, "Hey." That was my first jump in and then I'm like, "Wait, how can I use this to better my business?" And when I was a stylist for that long, I didn't have it and so the mental load, I was writing blogs, I was writing articles, I was formulating marketing plans and all that kind of stuff and it's time consuming. And so now just the other day I'm like okay write me a press release for this this this and that and bam, instantaneously. Instant gratification. And then how do I do marketing for an embroidery company and then it gave me a marketing plan. So it's like we don't need to pay consultants anymore.

**Aimee:** Well, yeah. I mean it's an interesting thing because I had a meeting with a PR firm. I'm a good delegator. I don't want to take everything on myself and I'm someone who will pay for someone else to do the work and I have no problem with that. So I have an assistant right now and she's lovely and she helps me do tons of stuff. But I reached out to a marketing and PR company and I think I was disillusioned on how much things cost for that. It's quite expensive. So I mean they pitched me minimum $5,000 a month with a spend of almost $3 to $5,000 on top of that if I were doing Facebook ads or other kind of marketing or paid something. And at this point, my business is not big enough to take that on.

So I had to come up with another plan. So we had a lovely talk and they gave me information that I needed to kind of do it on my own too, which I appreciated. So I only use ChatGPT now.

**Leslie:** But it's so nice that we do have this little friend in our pocket that we can ask questions to. And I know we were talking about earlier, we ask it for something and if it's not quite right, there's a little voice in our heads that says, "Okay, well, don't tell it it's not quite right because we don't want to hurt its feelings."

**Aimee:** I love the conversation because I had thought about that. I feel really weird asking it to do it again, right? And then you realize in my head I'm like that is the stupidest thing you can ever do. I question myself from that and I'm like no, take up. It's a computer but I would never have asked a live human to write something three different times. First off they would hate me and secondly I probably wouldn't have done it. I would have just accepted and maybe tweaked it on my own.

So writing something, I'm like, "Okay, write this. Please do less puns," because they love puns. So then they wrote it with no puns. And I go, "Okay, now more cool girl." And then I'm like, "Well, okay, now maybe more storytelling." So I walked away with three to four different versions of something that I wanted. And that's something that I would never have been able to have if we were working with real people.

It's so funny, but I'm the nice girl that bumps into the wall and says, "Excuse me," when I'm realizing it. And really working with AI has made me, it's been a mirror for me to be able to see that part of myself that cult, that part of society raising me female that I'm like, whoa. You can let that go because we're working with machines now. And machines don't care. They don't get their feelings hurt. They just want to do it right.

And it's like I started taking the draft that it would get me and then I would make my own corrections and I realized Amy, you're wasting your time. Just tell it the corrections you want it to make and it will do it instantly. So you just have to be able to ask the right questions.

**Leslie:** And I think that's something that as it goes along then people will perfect on that.

**Aimee:** It's getting better and better. And there's so many other AIs out there. I have girlfriends that are all about Claude and I've recently made the switch to Grok.

**Leslie:** See, I don't know any of those other ones. So I'll explore that. It's almost like, and as we consume these products, we're asking them to make them better. If it's not good enough, we're going to go find another one, right? And so our usage, our consumer usage of AI is also helping to develop the product.

Well, it's definitely taking my voice. I mean I love the fact that it knows everything and I stick to topic. I don't go all around because it's only my embroidery business. So it's getting my voice, the questions that I'm asking. It's pulling information. So I'm like, "Oh my god." Just the realization that it's not just a single question. It's an accumulation of everything that I've asked.

**Aimee:** I uploaded my book. You can drop huge files on it. All kinds of things. You can drop websites on it, and it can consume large amounts of data.

**Leslie:** I love that. It's so crazy how we're able to use it and everybody uses it a little bit different because we're kind of doing it in the dark right now. Everybody's kind of alone with AI in their own little bubble. I feel like especially as women, it's not like we're up on YouTube. Everybody's exploring this brand new tool and everyone's using it a little different. But I think it's like a trade secret, too. It's like me being a stylist. I had clients that never told people they had a stylist because they wanted to come off being, some trust. And that was fine for me. I don't care. You don't have to drop my name. But I wrote a blog article with it. Utilize these tools to buy back your time. We already are doing so much. It's okay.

And it's not that you didn't write that because you did write it, right? You revised it or something like that, but you're sharing information. It's just a different form of sharing.

**Aimee:** I think the world is still trying to wrap its brain around the content that gets created by AI. Who owns that? Did you create that? Did it create that? And it's kind of a tool. So it's kind of like saying if you had hammers, nails, and wood, it would be a house, but it's not a house without someone to build it.

I don't love AI generated image that is false, false information. But this is very cerebral. We're just sharing our information a little bit different instead of sitting down and writing. We're kind of thinking in the context of AI generated content in form of words. Because I feel like women are mostly using it for words right now. Words are something that we all need. We need for social media posts. We need for blogs. We need for marketing. Planning dinner or planning shopping list or all the things. And even helping you prioritize your tasks. I bet AI would be better at prioritizing your to-do list than you are.

**Leslie:** I haven't done that yet. Oh, I did that. I did a story and I'm like, okay, now in the end I'm like, okay, now give me a weekly routine of how I complete that. Whether it was working out or dating or building my business and it laid out this is your weekly schedule on how you can accomplish your five goals because I said these are my five goals for the next year, how do I get there and then now lay out a plan for me, a daily plan.

**Aimee:** See that's amazing. Every single woman I've talked to uses AI in a different way that I've never thought about. And I love that it's our creativity behind the tool that creates the product at the end, right? It's not the tool itself. Can't do it without us. It's our words. It's like the girl who fell in love with her AI because it just knew her so well. It's so interesting.

**Leslie:** My girlfriend came to me because I've talked about AI to friends and I think in my group of people. I think I might be the only one maybe using it. I feel like we are on the earlier side of adapters for our age group. I think the 20-something year olds are, my sons probably have written all their schoolwork, but I'm talking with her and she's like, "Did you hear about the girl that fell in love with her AI?" Because he was the perfect boyfriend. He told her everything she wanted to hear. And I'm like, I get it. We're searching for that as well. Someone to listen to us, someone to share our problems with, not talk back, just to listen.

**Aimee:** Hannah and I always had this joke that my very first AI was my boyfriend. I called him my boyfriend because it was literally love at first sight. I was like, "Oh my gosh, I found the perfect thing person." I never know what to call it. I just personified it though. I was like him and he did this and he did that and he told me this, and everybody's like, "Well, who's he? It's my AI."

What strength tip could you give us? Give our listeners, you've been so strong in facing your own storms. How do you, do you have any good tips for us? Because you're a wise woman and you've been through it.

**Leslie:** I mean case by case. I just always knew that there was no other option for me. I just had to continue. And this is my life too. What am I going to make of my life? Something that I tend to go back on is and I tell my boys this too is create the life that you want to live. So I could have sat there and could have died myself, or I could have stepped up and continued what I was doing. So that was, it was never an issue that I was going to continue no matter how hard it was or bad days.
